feed.title: Stories by Andrew Cruz  on Medium
feed.description: Stories by Andrew Cruz  on Medium

items.title: UCLA Computer Science: Reflections from a First-Year Student
items.author: Andrew Cruz
items.description:
As my freshman year at UCLA concludes, I’m excited to share my initial thoughts on being a student here, specifically what it’s like to study computer science. This article delves into my experiences with coursework, extracurricular activities, and the engineering culture at UCLA.

I really enjoyed my first year at UCLA, but there are a few things I wish I had known and prepared for before starting school here. In this article, I offer some tips and observations, such as strategies for course registration and club involvement, to help future Bruins navigate their own UCLA journey.

Note: My current major is “Mathematics of Computation,” a combined math and computer science major in the College of Letters & Science. This major has nearly identical lower-division requirements to the computer science major in the School of Engineering, and I plan to switch to that major soon.

Classes

One of the main differences between UCLA computer science and computer science programs at other large public universities is its emphasis on breadth. During my freshman year, I took a mix of math, physics, and computer science classes. Here’s what my schedule looked like:

Fall: Math 32A (multi pt.1), Physics 1A (mechanics), Sociology 1
Winter: Math 33A (linear algebra), Physics 1B (electrostatics), CS 31 (intro to C++)
Spring: Math 32B (multi pt.2), Math 33B (differential equations), Physics 1C (magnetism), CS 32 (more C++)

Most engineering students take three or four core classes per quarter, sometimes adding a lab or general education requirement. All of the classes I’ve taken, except for Sociology 1, are lower-division requirements for the computer science major. Looking back at my first year, I would say that the most memorable academic experience was taking the Physics 1 series.

Physics 1 Series

All UCLA computer science majors are required to take the Physics 1 series, which is notorious for being a GPA crusher. This requirement is quite unusual, as most computer science programs at other universities typically only require one physics class. At UCLA, however, computer science students must complete three physics classes in addition to a lab.

Personally, I found Physics 1A and Physics 1B to be my most challenging courses this past year. Despite taking AP Physics C: Mechanics in high school, I still struggled with the higher level of difficulty in Physics 1A. Later, I found out that you can actually petition the engineering department to bypass Physics 1A with a score of 4 or 5 on the AP Mechanics exam.

Physics 1B proved to be even more challenging. Not only was it completely new material (I hate circuits), but our professor was new, gave out weekly quizzes, and made the exams extremely complicated. Luckily, the class was single-handedly saved by our TA (god bless Rayner).

As a first-year student, it’s important to recognize that many lower-division requirements, such as the Physics 1 series, are “weeder” classes known for their rigor and grade deflation. However, the difficulty of these classes can also vary based on the professor and the class cycle.

Professors

Early on, I discovered how a good professor can transform a challenging class into a bearable (and sometimes even enjoyable) experience. Like many schools, UCLA has its own professor rating website: Bruinwalk. When I found a Math 33B professor known to have a 70% A rate, I rushed to sign up for their class section. Conversely, when I realized the only professor teaching Math 61 had a sub-2 star rating, I opted to take that course a different quarter.

Cycle

Taking classes “on-cycle” versus “off-cycle” can also make a huge difference. Most computer science freshmen choose to take CS 31 starting in fall quarter, CS 32 in winter, and CS 33 in spring — this is considered “on-cycle”. With this in mind, I decided to delay taking these classes by one quarter, not enrolling in CS 31 until winter quarter. Although enrolling “off-cycle” put me a quarter behind other students, it proved beneficial. I encountered more manageable professors and a less rigorous grading curve. While my “on-cycle” friends complained constantly about the workload in their computer science classes, I found CS 31 and CS 32 to be notably less challenging. I probably watched a grand total of three lectures across both classes, though I still spent 30+ hours on the infamous CS 32 project 3.

Course Selection

Navigating course registration at UCLA can be daunting. I remember my first time trying to enroll in classes, only to find out that most of my desired courses were already full. Just like other large public universities, UCLA has the problem of having way too many students for the number of spots offered in each class. This means in-demand classes can be very difficult to get, especially for younger students with later enrollment times. Here’s some of the strategies I’ve learned to manage this process better:

1. Identify Backup Classes: Having plenty of backup classes is a must before course registration. Going into my enrollment time, I try to have eight or more different classes in mind with the hope of just getting three or four of them. For example, when Math 61 filled up last quarter, I had Math 32B and Math 33B as backups, saving me from a scheduling nightmare.
2. Be Intentional with First Pass and Second Pass: If you aren’t familiar with the two-pass system, first pass is the enrollment times for your first 10 units (two classes). Second pass comes after all first pass times are done and lets you sign up for your remaining classes. Therefore, prioritizing in-demand classes during first pass will often increase your likelihood of getting them.
3. Utilize PTE and ECR: If all else fails, many students have found success with PTE (Petition to Enroll) and ECR (Enrollment Consideration Request), which are last-ditch methods for getting classes that are already full. These methods are generally unreliable and are dependent on the professor/department.

Computer Science Curriculum

Beyond what I have already covered, one thing that stood out about UCLA academics is its computer science curriculum. UCLA’s introductory computer science courses are taught in C++, and I would bet that the curriculum hasn’t changed in the last 20 years or so (also evidenced by the class website http://web.cs.ucla.edu/classes/spring24/cs32/syllabus.html this thing looks like it was made in the stone age).

Even though learning an aging programming language is not a problem in itself, I would argue that UCLA’s computer science introductory courses are outdated and too conceptual. Whereas my friends at other universities are learning industry-applicable skills and technologies, I am stuck learning how to implement a doubly linked list with a dummy head node. Which is to say not very useful or interesting at all. I know that UCLA’s classes become more practical down the line, but these are some of my observations so far.

Clubs

Outside of coursework, extracurricular activities were an important part of my first year at UCLA. Unlike high school clubs, which are often used as gimmicks for college applications, academic clubs at the university-level are a lot more serious and can be great resume boosters.

UCLA has a ton of computer science-focused clubs, from national organizations like ACM and IEEE to smaller groups like Creative Labs and LA Hacks. Two clubs that I am actively involved in are DevX and DataRes Consulting.

DevX is a project-focused club where we work in 8–12 member teams to develop full-stack apps. I am currently working on an app called Retune, which is trying to improve music exploration by combining location sharing with Spotify activity. Next year, I will be on the DevX board as developer lead, running our club’s dev intern program.

DataRes Consulting is also a project-based club, but we work with startups to provide data science and machine learning consulting services. In spring quarter, I worked on a team developing an AI chatbot for the startup Chelle AI.

Being involved in academic clubs has been a great way to explore new technical areas and get more project experience, which is essential for any computer science major. It has also provided opportunities to meet new people, especially students from different grades. With my involvement in social clubs as well, participating in extracurricular activities has become a core and fun part of my UCLA experience.

It’s better to be overly involved in clubs initially — you can always cut back later. Use your first few quarters to explore different clubs while balancing academics and social life. UCLA offers a wide range of clubs, so it can be challenging to keep up. As an incoming student, attend info sessions for clubs that interest you and make sure to visit the Enormous Activities Fair during Week 0. Good luck!

Some Issues

One thing that has always puzzled me is the competitiveness of many clubs. For example, DevX’s sub-5% acceptance rate meant that even highly qualified students face rejection. This level of competition often feels more intense than applying to the university itself. To make things worse, many of these clubs choose to brag about their low acceptance rates and rigorous selection processes.

From what I have seen, academic clubs often face one of two problems.

1. Club A has great professional opportunities but is super elitist and difficult to get into.

2. Club B is open to all students but is disorganized and doesn’t end up doing much.

ACM is an example of Club B at UCLA. ACM positions itself as a beginner-friendly club catering to a variety of technical interests, with different committees for competitive programming, app development, cloud computing, and more. In theory, this sounds great. In practice, this broad structure can lead to disorganization and resource constraints. For instance, some of my friends in the ACM AI committee were assigned to projects that never got off the ground, while others didn’t get assigned to a project at all.

That said, some of my friends had excellent experiences in other ACM committees, like ACM Cyber. While the broad structure of ACM can create hurdles, there are areas within the club where members can find valuable opportunities.

What does this mean? It highlights the importance of being proactive and making the most of the resources available. While navigating clubs at UCLA and other universities can be challenging, actively engaging with them can can lead to meaningful experiences and personal growth.

Engineering Culture

Engineering at UCLA, like many large public universities, is heavily male-dominated, especially in computer science. However, one thing that sets UCLA apart is its embrace of engineers with non-traditional backgrounds.

While I have met many students like myself who have long known they wanted to study engineering, I have also encountered quite a few who hadn’t decided on engineering until committing to UCLA. For example, a friend of mine spent most of high school focusing on pre-health extracurriculars. After being accepted to UCLA for electrical engineering, they pivoted to EE despite having no prior experience in the field.

This means the range of students studying engineering at UCLA is quite diverse. In each of my classes, for every top student — the super-genius curve breakers that I would struggle to keep up with even if I tried — there are two students who are just beginning to navigate this level of coursework. While students at UCLA are still heavily academically inclined, the culture here is not as competitive or rigorous as I expected

Note: this is likely influenced by my background coming from a competitive Bay Area high school.

Work-Life Balance

One of the reasons I chose UCLA over other universities was its promise of a fun college experience, and it has not disappointed. I’ve been pleasantly surprised to find that even among engineering students, maintaining a healthy work-life balance is highly valued here.

UCLA is home to a huge athletic culture, where playing sports and hitting the gym is common — even amongst nerds. People love to go out and eat. The beach is only a few miles away. As a tennis player, living in LA has been a particular treat. With the second-largest tennis tournament in the U.S. just a couple of hours away, I’ve had the opportunity to watch top tennis pros practice right on our UCLA courts (as well as trek out to the tournament itself). With so many things nearby, living in LA is a lot of fun, making it one of the major perks of attending UCLA.

With so many things nearby, living in LA is a lot of fun, making it one of the major perks of attending UCLA.

Some Problems

While I appreciate a healthy work-life balance, I’ve noticed that career-focused motivation is frequently underemphasized within the engineering community. Since arriving at UCLA, I haven’t experienced a significant push to further my professional ambitions, and much of the career-oriented work I’ve done has been self-initiated.

What do I mean by career-oriented work? For computer science majors, this can be developing personal projects, practicing leetcode problems, networking with connections, and filling out hundreds of internship applications.

Career Resources

UCLA’s career resources do not provide an environment conducive to advancing careers in engineering and technology. The career center offers minimal help, and anyone who has attended a UCLA career fair knows the frustration of waiting in long lines without even getting a chance to speak one-on-one with a recruiter. From what I’ve heard, the career center does a poor job of fostering connections with tech companies, making UCLA even less of a recruiting hotspot for engineering.

To make matters worse, UCLA is not a target school for computer science. While our program is relatively highly ranked (top 15), it places us behind top-tier programs like Stanford, Berkeley, and the University of Washington. As a result, UCLA students lose a competitive edge when it comes to tech recruitment.

In my experience, UCLA’s alumni network is also limited in the support it provides. Although I’ve managed to arrange some coffee chats and secure a few referrals from former Bruins, the majority of alumni don’t seem interested in offering assistance. Compared to a school like USC, which is renowned for its strong alumni network, UCLA’s network is not as competitive.

Not to say that it is impossible for computer science students at UCLA to be successful. A quick scroll on LinkedIn reveals plenty of UCLA students with FAANG+ internships. There are still incredibly motivated individuals at UCLA, but the overall environment doesn’t seem to foster a high level of career-focused ambition.

Research Opportunities

If there is one thing UCLA does well, it is making research opportunities accessible to self-motivated students. Every one of my friends who tried to join a research lab managed to secure a research position by cold emailing professors (unfortunately, the official research portal is unreliable). Often, this research ends up being tangential to their career goals, such as doing bioinformatics as a data science major.

Final Thoughts

While career-focused students may be disappointed by UCLA’s lack of resources, self-motivated students will still find plenty of opportunities here, whether that be through clubs, research, or local startups. UCLA might not be as competitive or demanding as a school like Berkeley, but its emphasis on work-life balance can help students excel in other areas.

Transferring to Computer Science

Quick little tidbit about switching majors: Transferring to computer science is common and doable, which is great news for students like me who didn’t initially apply to the School of Engineering. All you need is a 3.5 GPA, an engineering workload (taking a science class and a math class in the same quarter), and completion of CS 31, 32, and 33.

Conclusion

As I reflect on my freshman year, I’m grateful for the opportunities and challenges that have shaped my journey so far. Attending college has been a huge wake-up call in terms of time and priority management, and UCLA has definitely helped me become more independent. While I still take great care to excel in my academics, I also make sure to stay involved with both academic and social clubs. Despite the demands of my part-time internship at Lockheed, I make it a point to take every opportunity to go out and enjoy myself. I continue to prioritize side projects and learning new technical skills while also emphasizing the importance of staying physically active.

This year, I’ve made tons of friends and gotten to play way too much poker, leaving me with very little to complain about. While I have some qualms with the career resources here, I believe UCLA offers a fantastic environment for self-motivated students to thrive. The diverse experiences and challenges at UCLA have made my freshman year incredibly memorable. I’m very excited to return in the fall and see what the next three years will bring.

items.title: My Experience as a High School Intern at Lockheed Martin
items.author: Andrew Cruz
items.description:
Every high school student knows the stress of lining up summer opportunities, whether that be a job, volunteer work, a research position, or even that coveted internship. For those looking for a summer internship, you have probably already run into the qualification predicament. For most companies, a high school education is not enough for the job; most are looking for college students, specifically those from top universities. But even if they don’t, they’re looking for candidates with experience, and most high school students don’t fit the bill. Just like tons of other high school students, I asked myself “how can I get an internship if it requires experience that I can only get from this internship?” I need experience to get experience, but I can’t get that experience without prior experience. How does that even work?

Well, first of all, that is not necessarily true. But second of all, networking and a lot of luck. I am now an incoming freshman at UCLA, but I have been interning at Lockheed Martin since the summer after junior year of high school. So 15 months and counting. And while I didn’t get my internship through the grind of cold email networking en masse like some others, my relationship with my AP Computer Science teacher helped point me in the right direction.

As part of the computer science club at my high school, I was able to participate in the annual Codequest competition held by Lockheed Martin. Now if you’re familiar with competitive programming, Codequest is just another regional competitive programming competition with teams representing different high schools. And even though my team got its ass kicked (even with a USACO plat + gold on our team), the competition offered an internship application for all participants.

Tip #1: Resume! I would highly recommend all high school students to have some sort of resume prepared or at least one that’s in the works even if you don’t have any opportunities lined up yet. At the very least, it is good practice and will give you a leg up when you do start to look for opportunities.

The really great thing about this opportunity with Lockheed Martin was that they were looking for high school applicants specifically. So when it came to the interview, they cared a lot more about why I was interested in a technical field (for me computer science) and how I had pursued it (intellectual vitality) rather than previous industry experience (phew!). Luckily for me, I had plenty to talk about due to a variety of personal projects I had been working on for a few years prior. While these projects were nothing impressive on their own, they gave me an opportunity to show that I was invested in learning different aspects of computer science. My experience working on my own projects really helped carry me through that interview, ultimately landing me an internship offer later that week.

Tip #2: Personal projects! This applies specifically to computer science students but also can be adjusted to work for other students in STEM. After getting started with a coding language (or multiple coding languages) whether that be from a class at school or from an outside source, it is really important that you find some sort of project you are interested in working on. Not only is this a really great learning experience (hands-on learning!), having a portfolio of projects will look impressive to potential employers/mentors and will definitely help in landing an opportunity, especially if you have no other experience. While personal projects may seem daunting at first, there are a ton of resources on the internet to help you get started. It doesn’t hurt to start small.

Just some tidbits about the job itself. I am working remotely as is the rest of my team. Most of the people I work with live on the east coast, and the three hour time difference has proven to be difficult at times. There was also a 3 week period where I had to attend a 5am-10am training which was a bit of a pain (the training leaders were based in India I believe). My team is on a 4/10 work schedule, but it is not always expected to work the full 40 hours a week as an intern. There is lots of flex time based on the amount of work, which is really convenient. Also, I am the only intern on the team which is kind of interesting.

Probably the most difficult part of this internship was getting started. I was not told my start date until the night before, and my work computer was randomly shipped to me with no prior notice. My manager also had no idea that I was in a different time zone, and I remember receiving multiple calls on the first day at 5ish in the morning (I was still sleeping). But after this initial lapse of communication, the logistics of the internship were laid out well, and I had no related issues going forward. Technically speaking, my work computer was a pain to set up, but that probably goes with most jobs.

I also remember having a severe case of imposter syndrome getting started. In fact, after just a few days, I remember telling myself that I would not make it through the summer. And to be honest, the first few weeks were incredibly overwhelming. My team threw loads of information my way, from different documentation sources to a heap of development processes and softwares, all of which I spent over a hundred hours poring through.

Although the start was difficult, my “buddy” on the team was a world of help to me. I learned quickly to ask a ton of questions, schedule meetings with the team, and ask for demos and examples if I needed help. Being able to communicate difficulties I was having and asking questions really paid dividends for me. At this point, I have been working at Lockheed Martin for over a year on a few different projects, and I feel a lot more confident.

Tip #3: Ask for help! I think one unrealistic expectation as an intern is that you need to prove that you are qualified and you can do things on your own. In fact, the opposite is true. The whole point of an internship is to learn and to get experience. In that respect, some “hand-holding” is okay and even needed.

For those interested in the actual work I am doing, here’s a few technical tidbits:

Year 1: Java API development, Postman testing, GraphQL API development, API documentation automated generation

Year 2: eQubeMI development (eQube is a no/low-code software solution), Javascript API testing

Overall, I have been really happy about my internship experience at Lockheed Martin. It has been a great learning experience for me, and I really enjoy working with my team. The one qualm I might have is the pay; many of my friends end up earning a bit (sometimes a lot) more working other jobs, though they do not have the same resume value and experience potential as this internship. A worthy trade-off.

For anyone looking to follow this path, the first step is joining or even starting the Codequest team at your school. This probably means getting involved with your school’s computer science/STEM related club (something you should do either way!).

While I would highly recommend finding an internship like this, there are certainly other great opportunities I would consider. The following is a short list of summer activities that I have heard other students pursue:

1. Research position. This can be either through a program (there are tons for high school students) or through a university. Finding a professor to research under is definitely a more difficult approach but can also pay dividends. Great learning experience!

2. Paid job. Making some money over the summer can never hurt on top of gaining invaluable work experience. This can be related to one of your interests or not.

3. Volunteer work. Lots of opportunities out there, can be either in your immediate community or have a larger scope. Never hurts to be related to one of your interests.

4. Summer programs. There are a ton of different programs (many are paid) through various universities. Find a program related to one of your interests for maximum benefit.

5. Summer classes. Many students end up taking classes to get ahead in high school, knock out some GE requirements, or learn something they are interested in. Most classes are low-cost.

Whatever you decide to do with your summer is not meant to be overly stressful, though it may seem that way sometimes. Good luck!
items.content:
Every high school student knows the stress of lining up summer opportunities, whether that be a job, volunteer work, a research position, or even that coveted internship. For those looking for a summer internship, you have probably already run into the qualification predicament. For most companies, a high school education is not enough for the job; most are looking for college students, specifically those from top universities. But even if they don’t, they’re looking for candidates with experience, and most high school students don’t fit the bill. Just like tons of other high school students, I asked myself “how can I get an internship if it requires experience that I can only get from this internship?” I need experience to get experience, but I can’t get that experience without prior experience. How does that even work?

Well, first of all, that is not necessarily true. But second of all, networking and a lot of luck. I am now an incoming freshman at UCLA, but I have been interning at Lockheed Martin since the summer after junior year of high school. So 15 months and counting. And while I didn’t get my internship through the grind of cold email networking en masse like some others, my relationship with my AP Computer Science teacher helped point me in the right direction.

As part of the computer science club at my high school, I was able to participate in the annual Codequest competition held by Lockheed Martin. Now if you’re familiar with competitive programming, Codequest is just another regional competitive programming competition with teams representing different high schools. And even though my team got its ass kicked (even with a USACO plat + gold on our team), the competition offered an internship application for all participants.

Tip #1: Resume! I would highly recommend all high school students to have some sort of resume prepared or at least one that’s in the works even if you don’t have any opportunities lined up yet. At the very least, it is good practice and will give you a leg up when you do start to look for opportunities.

The really great thing about this opportunity with Lockheed Martin was that they were looking for high school applicants specifically. So when it came to the interview, they cared a lot more about why I was interested in a technical field (for me computer science) and how I had pursued it (intellectual vitality) rather than previous industry experience (phew!). Luckily for me, I had plenty to talk about due to a variety of personal projects I had been working on for a few years prior. My experience working on my own projects really helped carry me through that interview, ultimately landing me an internship offer later that week.

Not only is this a really great learning experience (hands-on learning!), having a portfolio of projects will look impressive to potential employers/mentors and will definitely help in landing an opportunity, especially if you have no other experience. While personal projects may seem daunting at first, there are a ton of resources on the internet to help you get started. It doesn’t hurt to start small.

Most of the people I work with live on the east coast, and the three hour time difference has proven to be difficult at times. There was also a 3 week period where I had to attend a 5am-10am training which was a bit of a pain (the training leaders were based in India I believe). My team is on a 4/10 work schedule, but it is not always expected to work the full 40 hours a week as an intern. There is lots of flex time based on the amount of work, which is really convenient. Also, I am the only intern on the team which is kind of interesting.

Probably the most difficult part of this internship was getting started. I was not told my start date until the night before, and my work computer was randomly shipped to me with no prior notice. My manager also had no idea that I was in a different time zone, and I remember receiving multiple calls on the first day at 5ish in the morning (I was still sleeping). But after this initial lapse of communication, the logistics of the internship were laid out well, and I had no related issues going forward. Technically speaking, my work computer was a pain to set up, but that probably goes with most jobs.

I also remember having a severe case of imposter syndrome getting started. In fact, after just a few days, I remember telling myself that I would not make it through the summer. And to be honest, the first few weeks were incredibly overwhelming. My team threw loads of information my way, from different documentation sources to a heap of development processes and softwares, all of which I spent over a hundred hours poring through.

Although the start was difficult, my “buddy” on the team was a world of help to me. I learned quickly to ask a ton of questions, schedule meetings with the team, and ask for demos and examples if I needed help. Being able to communicate difficulties I was having and asking questions really paid dividends for me. At this point, I have been working at Lockheed Martin for over a year on a few different projects, and I feel a lot more confident.

Tip #3: Ask for help! I think one unrealistic expectation as an intern is that you need to prove that you are qualified and you can do things on your own. In fact, the opposite is true. The whole point of an internship is to learn and to get experience. In that respect, some “hand-holding” is okay and even needed.

For those interested in the actual work I am doing, here’s a few technical tidbits:

Year 1: Java API development, Postman testing, GraphQL API development, API documentation automated generation

Year 2: eQubeMI development (eQube is a no/low-code software solution), Javascript API testing

Overall, I have been really happy about my internship experience at Lockheed Martin. It has been a great learning experience for me, and I really enjoy working with my team. The one qualm I might have is the pay; many of my friends end up earning a bit (sometimes a lot) more working other jobs, though they do not have the same resume value and experience potential as this internship. For anyone looking to follow this path, the first step is joining or even starting the Codequest team at your school. This probably means getting involved with your school’s computer science/STEM related club (something you should do either way!).

While I would highly recommend finding an internship like this, there are certainly other great opportunities I would consider. The following is a short list of summer activities that I have heard other students pursue:

1. Research position. This can be either through a program (there are tons for high school students) or through a university. Finding a professor to research under is definitely a more difficult approach but can also pay dividends. Great learning experience!

2. Paid job. Making some money over the summer can never hurt on top of gaining invaluable work experience. This can be related to one of your interests or not.

3. Volunteer work. Lots of opportunities out there, can be either in your immediate community or have a larger scope. Never hurts to be related to one of your interests.

4. Summer programs. There are a ton of different programs (many are paid) through various universities. Find a program related to one of your interests for maximum benefit.

5. Summer classes. Many students end up taking classes to get ahead in high school, knock out some GE requirements, or learn something they are interested in. Most classes are low-cost.

Whatever you decide to do with your summer is not meant to be overly stressful, though it may seem that way sometimes. Good luck!
